text: Two trocars are placed in the right lower quadrant, and a third trocar is positioned above the umbilicus for the camera. The sigmoid colon is visibly attached to the lateral abdominal wall, limiting the perforation. The surgeon begins dissection from the uncomplicated side, using a hook cautery to dissect the mesocolon.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery and ligates it with two hemoclips before cutting it. The left ureter is visible in the surgical field. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified near the ligament of Treitz, alongside a second venous structure resembling the renal vein, which is carefully removed from the dissection site. The mesenteric vein is ligated with hemoclips and cut using an energy device.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side, mobilizing the sigmoid colon and revealing the perforation. An intraoperative colonoscopy is performed to determine the resection margin for healthy colon mucosa, and the colon is transected using a stapler.

The surgeon performs a colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, ensuring the connection between the colon segments is secure. An air leak test is conducted, confirming the integrity of the anastomosis as negative for leaks.
